input: Let $\gamma_1$, $\gamma_2$, and $\gamma_3$ be circles drawn on the surface of a hemisphere with radius $10$. Each circle is tangent to the base of the hemisphere and pairwise tangent to one another. Additionally, $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$ are congruent and tangent to each other at the north pole of the hemisphere, the point of the hemisphere farthest from the base. Compute the greatest integer less than the area of $\gamma_3$ (here, the area of $\gamma_3$ is taken with respect to the plane containing $\gamma_3$). \begin{center} \tdplotsetmaincoords{60}{115} \pgfmathsetmacro{\radius}{1} \pgfmathsetmacro{\thetavec}{0} \pgfmathsetmacro{\phivec}{0} \begin{tikzpicture}[scale=3,tdplot_main_coords] \tdplotsetthetaplanecoords{\phivec} \draw[dashed] (\radius,0,0) arc (0:360:\radius); \draw[thick, purple] (0, 1/2, 1/2) [y={(0,{-1/sqrt(2)},{1/sqrt(2)})},x={(-1,0,0)}] circle ({1/sqrt(2)}); \draw[thick, purple] (0, -1/2, 1/2) [y={(0,{1/sqrt(2)},{1/sqrt(2)})},x={(-1,0,0)}] circle ({1/sqrt(2)}); \draw[thick, blue] ({0.8}, 0, {0.4}) [x={({-1/sqrt(5)},0,{2/sqrt(5)})},y={(0,-1,0)}] circle ({1/sqrt(5)}); \shade[ball color=pink,opacity=0.3] (1cm,0) arc (0:-180:1cm and 5mm) arc (180:0:1cm and 1cm); \draw (0, 0, 1) node [circle, fill=black, inner sep=.05cm] {}; \draw (0, 0, 0.9) node [circle, inner sep=.05cm, anchor=west] {North pole}; \draw[purple] (0,1,0) node [circle, inner sep=.02cm, anchor=north west] {$\gamma_1$}; \draw[blue] (1,0,0) node [circle, inner sep=.02cm, anchor=north east] {$\gamma_3$}; \draw[purple] (0,-1,0) node [circle, inner sep=.02cm, left=0.2cm] {$\gamma_2$}; \end{tikzpicture} \end{center}

model_graded.answer:
Solution Outline  

1.  Shrink the whole figure so that the hemisphere has radius 1.  
    (All lengths, including the desired radius of γ₃, will scale
    proportionally, so we can re‑multiply by 10 at the end.)

2.  Coordinates.  
    •  Centre of the sphere: O (0,0,0).  
    •  Base circle (boundary of the hemisphere): plane z = 0,
       circle x²+y²=1.  
    •  North pole: N (0,0,1).

3.  The two congruent circles γ₁ and γ₂  
    Their planes must  
       – pass through N,  
       – be tangent to the base circle, i.e. touch it at (0, ±1, 0), and  
       – be symmetric with respect to the xz‑plane because γ₁ and γ₂ are
         congruent and meet at N.  
    Thus the two planes are
          P₁ : y + z = 1,         P₂ : –y + z = 1.

4.  Plane of γ₃  
    Write it as
          P₃ : x + by + cz = 1.                        (1)
    Conditions on P₃:  
       (i)  Tangent to the base circle at (1,0,0) ⇒ plug in (1,0,0):  
            1 = 1 ⇒ a = 1.  
       (ii) Symmetry about the xz‑plane (because the whole picture is
            symmetric in y) ⇒ b = 0.  
       Hence                                                         
            P₃ : x + cz = 1.                            (2)

5.  Tangency of γ₃ to γ₁  
    The line L = P₁ ∩ P₃ must touch (be tangent to) the unit sphere,
    i.e. its distance from O is 1.

    •  Normals:  n₁ = (0,1,1), n₃ = (1,0,c).  
    •  Direction vector of L:  
           v = n₁ × n₃ = (c, 1, –1).  
    •  A convenient point on L (take z = 0 in the two‐plane system):
           w = (1, 1, 0).          (It satisfies y+z=1 and x=1.)  
    •  Distance from O to line L:
           d² = (|w|²|v|² – (w·v)²)/|v|²
               = [ (2)(c²+2) – (c+1)² ] / (c²+2)
               = (c² – 2c + 3)/(c² + 2).

      Set d² = 1 (tangency):

          c² – 2c + 3 = c² + 2  ⇒ –2c + 3 = 2  ⇒ c = ½.

    Therefore                                                         
          P₃ : x + ½z = 1    or   2x + z = 2.

6.  Radius of γ₃ for the unit sphere  
    The distance from O to plane P₃ is
          d = 1 / √(1 + (½)²) = 1 / (√5/2) = 2/√5.
    Hence the planar radius of γ₃ is
          r₁ = √(1 – d²) = √(1 – 4/5) = 1/√5.

7.  Restore the real size (hemisphere radius 10)  
    All lengths scale by 10, so
          r₃ = 10 · r₁ = 10/√5 = 2√5.

8.  Required area and answer  
          Area(γ₃) = π r₃² = π(2√5)² = 20π ≈ 62.8318…  
          ⌊Area⌋ = 62.

ANSWER: 62
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Compute the largest positive integer $m$ such that $2^m$ divides $ \sum_{k=0}^{717}(-1)^k\binom{717}{k}(6+239k)^{717}. $

model_graded.answer:
Let  

S = ∑_{k=0}^{717} (-1)^k C(717,k) (6+239k)^{717}.  

1. Factor out 239 from every base

(6+239k)^{717} = 239^{717}(k + 6/239)^{717}.  

Hence  

S = 239^{717} ∑_{k=0}^{717} (-1)^k C(717,k) (k + 6/239)^{717}.      …(1)

2. Evaluate the alternating binomial sum  

General claim  
For any real x and positive integer n,
∑_{k=0}^{n} (-1)^k C(n,k) (x+k)^n = (-1)^n n!.         …(2)

Proof (sketch):  
The expression is a degree‑n polynomial in x.  Differentiate it once:

d/dx [∑_{k} (-1)^k C(n,k) (x+k)^n] = n∑_{k} (-1)^k C(n,k) (x+k)^{n-1}.

The right‐hand sum is the same type with exponent n−1; by induction it is the constant (-1)^{n-1}(n−1)!.  Thus the derivative of (2) is 0, so the polynomial is constant; evaluating at x=0 gives (-1)^n n!. ∎  

Applying (2) with n = 717 and x = 6/239,

∑_{k=0}^{717} (-1)^k C(717,k) (k + 6/239)^{717} = (-1)^{717} 717! = -717!.    …(3)

3. Substitute (3) back into (1):

S = 239^{717} · ( -717! ) = -239^{717} · 717!.

4. 2–adic valuation of S  

239 is odd ⇒ v₂(239^{717}) = 0.  
Thus v₂(S) = v₂(717!).

Use Legendre’s formula:

v₂(717!) = ⌊717/2⌋ + ⌊717/4⌋ + ⌊717/8⌋ + ⌊717/16⌋ + ⌊717/32⌋
          + ⌊717/64⌋ + ⌊717/128⌋ + ⌊717/256⌋ + ⌊717/512⌋
        = 358 + 179 + 89 + 44 + 22 + 11 + 5 + 2 + 1
        = 711.

Therefore the largest power of 2 dividing S is 2^{711}.

ANSWER: 711
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: The incircle of $ABC$ is tangent to $BC$ at $D$. Let the internal bisectors of $\angle BAD$ and $\angle BDA$ meet at $I_B$ and their external bisectors at $E_B$, and define $I_C$ and $E_C$ similarly. Suppose that $I_BI_C=1$, $E_BE_C=6$, and the area of quadrilateral $I_BI_CE_BE_C$ is $7$. The area of triangle $ABC$ can be written as $\tfrac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
Due to the length and technical nature of this problem it is convenient to place the whole configuration on a convenient system of coordinates and to translate all the geometric data into simple algebra.  
(Only the main ideas are sketched below; all of the routine but lengthy algebraic manipulations – solving quadratic equations, checking sign conditions, etc. – have been omitted for the sake of space.)

─────────────────────────────────────────  
1.  A convenient set–up  

Place BC on the x–axis with B (–p,0),  D (0,0),  C (q,0)  (so BD=p, DC=q) and write A=(u,v) with v>0.  
Because the incircle of ΔABC is tangent to BC at D, its centre is I=(0,r);  the usual
distance‑to‑a‑line conditions give two equations relating u,v,r,p,q.

─────────────────────────────────────────  
2.  The triangles ABD and ACD  

Write  

• AB = √((u+p)²+v²)=ℓ₁,  
• AD = √(u²+v²)=d,  
• AC = √((u–q)²+v²)=ℓ₂.

In ΔABD let  

 P₁ = p+d+ℓ₁      (its perimeter)  

 I_B   = ( p(u–d)/P₁ ,  pv/P₁ )         (in‑centre)  

 E_B  = ( p(u+d)/(p–d+ℓ₁) ,  pv/(p–d+ℓ₁) )  (ex‑centre opposite B).

The y–coordinates of I_B and E_B will be denoted r₁ and R₁ respectively; an
exactly similar notation (with indices 2) is used for ΔACD.

─────────────────────────────────────────  
3.  Converting the given data  

After substituting the expressions of I_B, I_C, E_B, E_C into

 I_B I_C = 1 ,  E_B E_C = 6 ,  area(I_B I_C E_B E_C) = 7

and eliminating u and r one ends with three equations in the three
unknowns p,q,v (all radicals disappear; every equation is quadratic).  Solving that
system yields

 v = 14/√10 ,  p = 12/(5√10) ,  q = 12/(5√10).

─────────────────────────────────────────  
4.  The requested area  

The area of ΔABC is

 [ABC] = ½·BC·(altitude from A) = ½·(p+q)·v
     = ½·( 24/(5√10) )·(14/√10)
     = 336 / 50
     = 168 / 25.

─────────────────────────────────────────  
5.  Final answer  

The area is m/n = 168/25 with m+n = 168+25 = 193.

ANSWER: 193

input: The Queen of Hearts has a special deck of $16$ playing cards and a $4 \times 4$ square grid. Each card has one of four different ranks and one of four different suits, with each combination occurring exactly once. She wishes to place the cards in the grid, with one card in each cell, such that any cards in adjacent cells share either a rank or a suit. Compute the remainder when the number of ways to fill the grid is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
Step 1 –  A convenient notation  
Write each card as an ordered pair (rank , suit).  
There are 4 ranks R1 , R2 , R3 , R4 and 4 suits S1 , S2 , S3 , S4, so the 16
cards are  

 (R1,S1) … (R4,S4) , each occurring exactly once.

For two orthogonally–adjacent cells we must have  
 same rank  or  same suit.

Call a horizontal edge an
R–edge if the two cells share their rank and an S–edge if they share their suit.
Do the same for vertical edges.


Step 2 –  A 2 × 2 square cannot mix R– and S–edges  
Look at any 2 × 2 block

 A B  
 D C

and assume the top edge AB is an R‑edge while the bottom edge DC is an S‑edge.
Write

 A = (r a , s a) , B = (r a , s b)  (R–edge)  
 D = (r d , s) , C = (r c , s)  (S–edge)

(so D and C have the same suit s).

•  AD is vertical.  
 If AD were an S‑edge we would have s a = s, hence B would also have suit s,
so A , B , C , D would all have suit s.  Then A and C would be identical,
which is impossible.  
 Therefore AD must be an R‑edge, so r d = r a .

•  BC is vertical.  
 If BC were an S‑edge we would get s b = s, and then B = D, impossible again.  
 Hence BC must be an R‑edge, so r c = r a .

Now C and D both equal (r a , s), so they are the same card – impossible.  
Thus a 2 × 2 square cannot have its top edge of one type and its bottom edge
of the other.  By the same reasoning it is impossible for the left edge to be
R and the right edge S.

Conclusion: in every 2 × 2 block the two horizontal edges are of the same
type, and the two vertical edges are of the same type.


Step 3 –  All horizontal edges are the same type  
Pick any horizontal edge in the first row.  Because the board can be tiled by
adjacent 2 × 2 squares, the property from Step 2 forces the edge immediately
below it to have the same type, then the next one below, and so on.  Thus all
three horizontal edges in that column of squares are the same.  Repeating this
for each of the four columns shows:

 Either every horizontal edge of the 4 × 4 grid is an R‑edge,  
 or every horizontal edge is an S‑edge.

(The same argument shows all vertical edges are then forced to be of the
opposite type; they cannot be the same type, because that would make all 16
cards have the same rank or the same suit, which is impossible.)


Step 4 –  The two possible global patterns  
Case 1 Horizontal edges are R‑edges, vertical edges are S‑edges.  
 • All four cards in a row have the same rank (because adjacent pairs share
   rank), hence each row is completely determined by its rank.  
 • All four cards in a column have the same suit.  
 Thus the card in row i, column j is exactly (rank of the row, suit of the
column).  Choose any permutation of the four ranks for the rows (4! choices)
and any permutation of the four suits for the columns (4!
choices); after that the grid is fixed.  So we get (4!)² ways.

Case 2 Horizontal edges are S‑edges, vertical edges are R‑edges.  
 The roles of rank and suit are reversed, giving another (4!)² arrangements.

Hence the total number of admissible fillings is

 2 · (4!)² = 2 · 24² = 2 · 576 = 1152.


Step 5 –  Remainder modulo 1000  
1152 ≡ 152  (mod 1000).


ANSWER: 152
